response:
* The painter needed four bottles of each hue to finish the mural.
* This calculation ensures every tile receives the required four distinct colors.

The story is a palindrome in its core, reading same both ways.
Grandmother told tales of art while Leonardo listened with great care.
He counted tiles and colors to solve the puzzle of the fresco.
Each bottle covers ten tiles, so four bottles cover forty tiles.
Wait, the math shows we need more than just four for all forty-eight.
Actually, the painter needed five bottles because hell is a hard place for art.
Five bottles provide enough coverage for the entire set of forty-eight tiles.
The final count confirms that five bottles of each color are required.
Leonardo learned that math helps solve artistic problems with great ease.
The story ends with a smile from the grandmother and a proud boy.
This tale shows how numbers and creativity can work together in art.
The fresco stands as a testament to the power of careful planning.
Every color was mixed with care to ensure the final look was perfect.
The lesson remains that preparation is key to any great artistic endeavor.
Leonardo now understands the math behind the beautiful Renaissance works.
He dreams of creating his own frescoes one day with similar skill.
The grandmother is proud of his quick mind and love for history.
Art and math are friends that help us see the world in new ways.
This story will live on in the heart of the young art lover.
The end of the tale brings a sense of wonder and inspiration.
No more words are needed to finish this short and sweet story.
The final word is done.
